arabic_text:
فَلَيْتَكَ تَحْلُو وَالحَيَاةُ مَرِيرَةٌ
وَلَيْتَكَ تَرْضَى وَالأَنَامُ غِضَابُ
وَلَيْتَ الَّذِي بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ عَامِرٌ
وَبَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ العَالَمِينَ خَرَابُ
إِذَا نِلْتُ مِنْكَ الوُدَّ فَالكُلُّ هَيِّنٌ
وَكُلُّ الَّذِي فَوْقَ التُّرَابِ تُرَابُ
فَإِنْ كَانَ شُرْبِي مِنْ وِدَادِكَ صَافِيًا
وَشُرْبِي مِنْ مَاءِ الفُرَاتِ سَرَابُ
alt_text: Arabic poem (four verses in the kamil meter): 'Would that you were sweet though life is bitter / and that you were content though humanity is angered / and that what is between me and you were prosperous / and what is between me and the worlds were ruined / if I gain from you affection then all else is easy / and all that is above the earth is dust / and if my drink from your love is pure / then my drink from the waters of the Euphrates is a mirage.'
notes: Famous Sufi/devotional verses — most often attributed to Rabi'a al-'Adawiyya or quoted in mystical literature.
